assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the original function of this large vase in ancient Greece?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/67c6932c9912e041348a5002e83e6e0c.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This amphora was found in a cemetery near the Dipylon Gate in Athens.

In ancient Greece, large vases, called amphorae, were used as grave markers like tombstones today.  

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-greece/v/dipylon-vase)**

choices.content: It was a grave marker in a cemetery.
It was a storage vessel for grain.
It was used to hold decorative flowers.
It was a cremation urn that held human remains.
It was part of a gateway to Athens.

hints:
Made from fine clay and water, slip is applied before firing and becomes visible after the firing process is complete.  

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-greece/v/dipylon-vase)**

choices.content: fine clay mixed with water used to decorate pottery
paint that is applied to a pot after firing
a large oven used to fire ceramic pottery
a type of ceramic glaze used for decoration

assessment_items.question_content:
What indicates that this is a funerary scene?  

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c89a2618673c52e402de2ce59c004b7a.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
No text appears on this amphora.

The central woman lies on a funeral bier, and is surrounded by figures pulling out their hair in a traditional gesture of mourning. 

Artists typically represented women in skirts to distinguish them from male figures, but this alone does not indicate that this is a funerary scene.

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-greece/v/dipylon-vase)**

choices.content: the figures are pulling at their hair
the text written on the vase

assessment_items.question_content:
**Greek artists created black figures on a red background using a sophisticated technique.  Place the following steps in slipwear in the correct sequence?**

[[☃ orderer 1]]
hints:
After the pot is formed on a wheel, slip is applied to decorate the clay vessel, which is then fired in a kiln.  The firing process involves first withdrawing oxygen from the kiln, which turns the pot black, and then adding oxygen back in, which causes the unpainted areas to return to their natural reddish color.  

**[Watch this video on making Greek pottery.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-greece/v/dipylon-vase)**

correct_options: the pot is formed on a wheel
slip is applied to the pot
the pot is placed in a kiln
oxygen is withdrawn from the kiln
oxygen is added to the kiln
figures are visible on the pot

assessment_items.question_content:
**What do you see in this detail that demonstrates the object was made during the LATE Geometric Period of Greek art?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9dc9aee98177ce53f1bd12c134064c19.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Vase painters during the Geometric Period often covered the entire surface with bands of geometric patterns and decorative motifs.

Repeated patterns made with animal and human forms first began to emerge In the late Geometric Period. Their appearance on the neck of this vessel helps date it to this time.  

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-greece/v/dipylon-vase)**

choices.content: the repeated animal forms in the decoration
the decoration covers almost all the surface
